steps.text: Thoroughly test the methods.
Peer review the methods.
Publish the methods as EPA methods.
Include the sampling procedures in the validation tests.
Clearly and completely state the measurement-process components validated and the combination of procedures composing a method.
Evaluate the uncertainty of each input quantity (method parameter) that may contribute significantly to the total uncertainty.
Estimate method bias by measuring materials whose composition is reasonably well known, comparing results to those from at least one alternate method or procedure, or analyzing spiked materials.

Consider the standard uncertainties of all input quantities (counting uncertainty, detection efficiency, chemical yields, interferences, decay corrections, sample size, etc.) when calculating the MQC of a method.
Verify the estimated MDC by testing materials containing analyte at the claimed detection level.
Evaluate method trueness and precision at the extremes of the analyte concentration range.
Evaluate method specificity by testing appropriate matrix blanks and fortified (spiked) matrix blanks.
Test a worst-case sample matrix during both intra- and interlaboratory validation studies.
Study actual samples of material containing an undetermined amount of the inherent radionuclide to evaluate method selectivity.
Use multiple analytical procedures during an intralaboratory validation study to substantiate the specificity of the method.
Evaluate the method uncertainty.
Evaluate trueness using samples that span the degree of analytical difficulty encompassed by the specified scope and applicability.
Evaluate the method for both repeatability (within-lab) and reproducibility (among labs).
Verify MQC estimates by analyzing samples containing radionuclide at the claimed MQC level.
Characterize analytical measurement uncertainty over an analyte concentration range.
Evaluate method ruggedness during method validation.
Include the design and results of the interlaboratory study in the method validation summary report.
Report data from the interlaboratory study in tabular form.
Include a discussion describing the details of, and rationale for, any changes made to the method resulting from the interlaboratory study.

preconditions.text: Sampling procedures are part of the method (e.g., methods for radon in air analysis and for volatile radioactive organic compounds in soils and other solid matrices).
The laboratory is able to predict standard uncertainties for measurements at specified levels of activity.
If adequate representations of matrix blanks cannot be obtained or prepared
Ensure there is an existing equation or procedure for evaluating the uncertainty of each measurement made using the method.
If the method is designed for demonstrating compliance with regulatory requirements and it is practical to do so.
Detection capability is relevant to the method scope and applicability.

constraints.text: For most radiological projects having regulatory limits for radioactive materials, a required method uncertainty at an action level (i.e., regulatory limit for the radionuclide) is considered essential to assure the quality of data used in statistical analyses and hypothesis testing.
The relative standard deviation of the measurement does not exceed a specified value, usually 10%.
An analyte concentration range must include either a regulatory limit or a defined action level, typically near the midpoint of the range of radionuclide activity level (concentration) for a project.
Matrix blanks should contain the chemical species and inherent radionuclides (other than the radionuclide of interest) reasonably expected to be present in an actual sample.
